title: 万圣节前夜的谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\126-万圣节前夜的谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
While walking through the hillside gardens of Stone Court Manor, Hercule Poirot observes the meticulous design of the landscape, which features rare shrubs and well-maintained paths equipped with comfortable seating. Poirot reflects on the beauty of the scenery and recalls a conversation regarding a murder that occurred years ago in an old quarry near the site. He notices a silhouette in the bushes and encounters a tall, slender, and exceptionally handsome man in his late thirties or early forties.

The man introduces himself as Michael Garfield, the architect of the garden. Garfield explains that the current owners, a retired Colonel and his wife, purchased the unprofitable house at a low price. Garfield reveals that the late Mrs. Llewellyn-Smith, who financed the garden, left the property to him in her will, though he subsequently sold the house and the Stone Mine Garden.

During their conversation, Poirot questions Garfield about his motives and his relationship with his former patron. Garfield admits that while he promised to follow Mrs. Llewellyn-Smith's instructions and views, he actually implemented his own ideas by presenting them as her own plans. He compares this professional tactic to selling a brown egg to a child by framing it as a premium rural product rather than just an egg. Garfield asserts that to be a successful artist and businessman, one must skillfully sell their own ideas through the guise of a client's blueprints.
